text: Quick check-in from PT this morning at Clínica Querétaro. Dra. Reyes had me grinding through those single-leg bridges again, the ones I hated last time, plus a new one with the resistance band that I'm pretty sure she invented to hurt me specifically. Honestly though — and this is the headline — the back is maybe 50% better than where it was. Not zero pain, still tightens up after long sitting at standup or in long meetings, but the constant low-grade ache that haunted me through month 11 is mostly gone. I can sit through standup without shifting every five minutes, and that alone is huge. I'll take it as a partial win and keep going.

OK so the cedar is finally settled in the apartment after dragging it back from El Roble in Toluca, and I'm staring at it like — qué onda, what now. This is genuinely the first time I've ever tried to build anything out of wood that isn't IKEA, and I'm finding out in real time that there's a whole vocabulary I don't have. Janka hardness. Quartersawn vs flatsawn. Reading the grain. Closed-pore vs open-pore. None of these are concepts I held in my brain three weeks ago, and now I have to know them to avoid screwing up a piece of cedar that cost me 3500 pesos.

I'm watching Paul Sellers videos at 2x speed and pretending I understood what he meant by 'reading the grain.' My only previous shop class memory is from secundaria in Guadalajara, and that was making a wooden spoon that ended up looking like a shoe. The teacher gave me a sympathy 7 out of 10 because he liked my dad. I have spent the last two and a half decades writing software, where the material doesn't fight back. Wood fights back. Wood has opinions about how it wants to be cut, and if you ignore those opinions it splits or tears or twists when humidity changes.

The Festool TS 55 REQ is honestly intimidating sitting on the floor of the maker space — it's got more buttons than my espresso machine, and I keep watching the safety video because I really do not want to lose a finger before the black belt test. The Veritas chisels still have factory edges and I've already nicked one against a screw I forgot to pull out of an offcut. Rookie mistake. The chisel survived. My pride did not.

After the wrong-grain disaster last week — where I'd cut and laminated four squares with the grain running the wrong way and the visual was just wrong, like you could tell something was off without being able to name it, the patterns didn't flow naturally — I'm going slower. Way slower. I'm measuring three times before each cut, drawing on the wood with a marking knife instead of a pencil so the line is consistent, and putting masking tape down on the cut line to reduce tear-out on the cedar's softer grain. Camila keeps walking past with that face like 'sí mi amor, the sawdust is everywhere again.' Anyway. First-time woodworker problems. Got any pointers on flattening boards by hand vs sending them through the planer at the Roma Norte maker space? I'm leaning toward hand because I want to learn, but the deadline is real — Hugo's birthday is six weeks out and I have a lot of joinery still to figure out. Tradeoff between learning and shipping. Same calculus I face at work every quarter.

I went to El Roble in Toluca with Pablo last weekend specifically for this — Pablo wanted to see the lumberyard because he's been considering taking up some woodworking himself, and it was a good excuse for me to go pick walnut for the inlay. The yard is on the highway about an hour from CDMX. The owner Don Manuel showed us the back stockroom where they keep the boards that aren't on display, the ones reserved for clients who know to ask. Pablo asked if there's something specific he should know about choosing wood for a first project. Don Manuel said 'don't pick by appearance, pick by stability' and showed Pablo how to look at the end grain to assess how the board will move. Pablo took notes. I liked watching it. Don Manuel reminded me a little of Profe — old craftsman teaching the next-generation guy by showing rather than explaining. Same teaching philosophy in different domains.

Funny thing about this chess board — I keep thinking about Hugo holding it. Not the finished thing, but the act of receiving. He's been so quiet since Beatriz died, like the version of him I met five years ago took 30% of his volume and put it in a closet somewhere and threw away the key. Camila says he plays chess against the computer at night because the silence in the São Paulo apartment is too much, and the computer is at least a presence that responds, that thinks back, that exists in the room with him at 11pm when the building is asleep.

He used to play with Beatriz on Sundays. Not because she was good — she'd lose in twelve moves and laugh about it, accuse him of cheating, demand a rematch — but because the ritual of it filled the afternoon. Two cafezinhos, a small bowl of cashews, the chess set on the dining table that's still in the apartment, untouched since the funeral. Camila told me last week that the position is still set up from their last game, eight months ago, and Hugo can't bring himself to put the pieces away.

So what I want, with this thing I'm building, is for the board to be heavy in his hands. I want the cedar to smell when he opens the box on his birthday. I want the squares to be a little uneven in places so he knows it was made by hand, by his daughter's stupid Mexican boyfriend who has never built anything before in his life, with tools the boyfriend bought specifically for this project and probably won't use much again afterward. I want the magnetic chess pieces to click into place with a small sound that's different from his current set's wood-on-wood. I want it to be unmistakably new, unmistakably his, unmistakably made for him. So that when he sits down to play in São Paulo at 11pm, he's not playing on the old board anymore — he's playing on a board that exists because he's still here, that exists in the present tense, that points forward instead of backward.

My own dad plays chess too, that's who taught me as a kid in Guadalajara, but Hugo plays the way you read poetry — slow, looking at every move twice, like the position itself is the point and winning is incidental. Beatriz used to make fun of him for taking forever between moves. Anyway. Beginner's mind on the wood. Going to attempt the inlay tomorrow with the walnut. If I screw it up I'll have to drive back to Toluca for more, and Camila will not be amused at another solo-Sebastián road trip in La Torta listening to vinyl rips on a USB stick at high volume.

The maker space charges 1,800 pesos a month for unlimited access plus tool insurance. Cheaper than I expected. The tool collection is more comprehensive than I expected too — they have a Festool TS55 track saw that I'd been eyeing for purchase but couldn't justify spending the money on for a one-off project, plus a Hammer K3 jointer-planer combo, plus a small Powermatic mortiser that's been waiting for a project I don't yet have. I joined for the rest of the chess board project plus probably the next two projects after if I find them. Camila pointed out that 1,800 pesos a month is less than I spend on yerba mate. Fair point. Cheaper hobby than I make it sound.

Tangential rust thing while I think about it. Remember the memory regression we hit a few months back, the Tokio task spawning leak? I want to make sure we don't reintroduce it as we onboard the new endpoints with Andrés. I went back through the post-mortem doc tonight and I'd kind of forgotten how subtle it was — we were spawning a detached task per request to do warm-cache lookups, and because the futures held a reference to the request body buffer, the buffer never got dropped until the task completed, and the task was awaiting on a channel that was occasionally never sent on if the upstream client cancelled the request. Classic leak. Subtle because the leak only kicked in under specific traffic shapes — clients that hung up early, mostly mobile clients on flaky LTE in Mexico City. Took us two weeks to even reproduce it.

The fix at the time was tying the warm-cache lookup to the request's tracing span lifetime via a tokio_util::CancellationToken so dropping the request future also propagated cancellation into the spawned task. We added a metric for `runtime.tasks.alive` exposed via tokio-metrics so we could watch it over time. The chart looked great after the fix — flat line where before there was a slow upward creep. I want to add a similar metric on the new dispatch endpoint Andrés is writing. He's keen but he's already gotten fancy with try_join_all spawning patterns, and I worry. I'm going to pair with him Monday morning, walk him through the post-mortem, then walk him through the fix.

The other thing I want to do — and tell me if this is overengineering — is set up a soak test. Like, low constant traffic, 50 RPS, run for 24 hours in CI on a nightly, and watch heap RSS plus the alive-tasks gauge plus the spawn_blocking active-threads count. We don't have it now, we just do burst load tests. Burst tests catch CPU saturation but not slow-leaking memory or task accumulation. Worth it for an extra hour of CI time per night? I think yes. The CI cost is a few cents of compute. The cost of a memory leak in production at 3am is a small career-shaped hole in my week.

Carla's coming back from parental leave next week and I want to surface this to her before she has to fight ghosts she didn't know existed. Anyway pasting the relevant code from the post-mortem repo because I'm half-thinking through it as I write — the broken version was `tokio::spawn(async move { let cached = warm_cache_lookup(&route_id).await; let _ = tx.send(cached); })` which leaked the request body through the spawned task. The fix wraps it in a `tokio::select!` racing the cancellation token, which means the spawned task gets cancelled cleanly when the parent request future is dropped, releasing the body buffer and any other request-scoped state. Does that pattern still hold up to you, or has the consensus moved? I keep seeing people use JoinSet now, which has nicer ergonomics for collecting results but I'm not sure it changes the cancellation story. Looking at tokio's source, JoinSet aborts on drop which is exactly what we want, so maybe I should just migrate everything to JoinSet and call it modern.

Camila came home tonight to find the kitchen smelling like cedar shavings (I'd been doing rough sanding at the apartment between maker space sessions because some of the work doesn't need the heavy equipment) and I expected her to be annoyed but she just said 'it smells like a cabin' and went to take her shoes off. She's been more tolerant of the chess board takeover of various surfaces in the apartment than I would have predicted. The dining table currently has a corner that's a chess board project zone, the entryway has the bag of clamps, the bedroom closet has the half-finished boards stacked vertically against the back wall waiting for the maker space's flat surface for the next gluing session. I'm grateful she's letting this take over without comment. I'd be less tolerant if she did the equivalent in the apartment.

Quick aside — the woodworking is doing weird things to my body. Different muscles than kickboxing. My forearms are sore from chiseling, my shoulders ache from holding the plane steady at the right angle for hours, but the back was actually fine after two hours hunched over the bench, which is wild because Wednesday class still wrecked it. PT says it's probably because I'm braced isometrically over the bench instead of twisting through hips like in kickboxing. Bench work as accidental physical therapy. New theory I'm testing on my own body.

OK the load test today was — bad. Not memory bad, this is different, and I've been staring at it for six hours and I think I need to write it out to make sense of it before I lose the thread.

We bumped up to a full burst profile to see how the new dispatch endpoint behaves at peak — 1200 RPS for 90 seconds, then idle for 20 seconds, then again, six cycles total. p50 stayed under 30ms throughout which is fine, but p99 went to 4.2 seconds. Four point two SECONDS. On a service that has a 38ms p95 in the easy case. And what's worse, p99 stayed elevated for like 20 seconds *after* the burst stopped. Which means we're queuing somewhere internal and not draining for a while after the load goes away. That post-burst tail is the smoking gun for queueing-without-progress.

The flame graph is showing — and this is the weird part — almost nothing on CPU during the spikes. Like 18% utilization on an 8-core box. So it's not CPU bound. The async runtime is just sitting there. Idle CPU and high latency at the same time. That's a fingerprint. If you're CPU bound, you're at 100% utilization with high latency. If you're IO bound, you're at low CPU with high latency but you'd see network or disk metrics climbing. Neither of those. The CPU is idle, the network is fine, the disk is fine, the database is fine, and yet requests are queueing somewhere inside our process.

Andrés ran it twice thinking it was a fluke, same result both times. Same shape. Same recovery time. Same lack of CPU usage. Reproducible. Good — reproducibility means we can debug it.

My first instinct was sqlx pool exhaustion, but the pgbouncer side is fine — connection counts steady at 12, no waiting count, no connection churn, no slow queries reported by the postgres slowlog. So it's not Postgres, definitively. Not Redis either, Redis CPU on the cluster is flatlined and the slowlog has nothing in it, average command latency is 0.4ms which is its normal floor. Network looks fine — kernel TCP retransmit counts normal, no buffer fills, no stuck connections in netstat. So it's something inside our process.

I'm now suspecting we're starving the Tokio runtime. We ship with `#[tokio::main]` default which on the version we're pinned to is current_thread flavor. Single-threaded executor, one OS thread doing all async work. So if any of our handlers — and I haven't found the culprit yet — does a CPU-bound bit of work without yielding, like a serialization pass or a regex compile or god forbid a synchronous file read or a tight numeric loop — we block the whole runtime for everyone else for the duration of that work. Under burst load that explains the queuing (everyone waits for the offending task), the lack of CPU usage (because most of the cores are idle, only one is being used), and the long tail (the queue takes time to drain even after the burst ends).

I've started running with RUST_LOG=trace,tokio=trace,runtime=trace and dumping to a file. The trace logs are massive — like 800MB per minute under load — but I can already see the executor parking and unparking in patterns that look bursty rather than steady. Long stretches with no scheduling, then a sudden batch of task wake-ups all at once. Classic starvation pattern. The tokio runtime is parking the worker thread because it has nothing schedulable, then unparking when a future becomes ready, and when it unparks it processes a burst of accumulated wake-ups all at once. That's not normal operation. Normal operation is steady polling with regular scheduling.

Tomorrow I'm going to instrument tokio-console which I keep meaning to set up properly. There's a `console-subscriber` crate that integrates with the tracing infrastructure and gives you a live view of every task, its state, how long it's been polling, idle, waiting, etc. Should make the offender obvious within 30 seconds of running once it's wired in. The wiring itself is maybe an hour of work — add the dependency, configure the subscriber, expose the console port on a non-default address since we're inside a k8s cluster, port-forward to my laptop.

Question for you: when you've debugged this kind of starvation pattern before, do you go straight to tokio-console or is there a poor-man's version with just the trace logs that's faster? I want to avoid a half-day yak shave on tracing infra if I can confirm the diagnosis with logs alone. But if tokio-console is the tool, I want to know that and just commit to setting it up properly. The trace logs are usable but they're noisy and I'm doing pattern-matching by eye, which doesn't scale.

Also — the move to multi_thread runtime feels obvious as a band-aid but I want to understand the offending handler first, because I think we have a logic bug masquerading as a runtime config bug. If I just slap multi_thread on it, the bug stays — it just gets harder to trigger because you'd need 4 simultaneous CPU-bound calls instead of 1. Eventually we'd hit it again at higher scale, in a worse situation, with less context. So: diagnose first, fix second. The multi_thread switch might still be the right architectural answer, but I want to make that decision deliberately, not as a panic response.

The other concern I have is the test infrastructure itself. Our k6 harness might be reporting weird p99s if the harness itself is overloaded — k6 is fine but at 1200 RPS we're driving from a single VM with limited CPU, and if k6 is queuing on its own side it'll show up as latency on our side falsely. I asked Andrés to verify with `k6 stats` that we're not VU-saturated. He confirms preAllocatedVUs is 200 and we're never hitting maxVUs of 800 during the run, so the harness has slack. Good. Means the latency is real and we own it.

Going to sleep on it. Tomorrow morning, fresh eyes, tokio-console. If I don't have a culprit by lunch I'm escalating to Carla early — she's back next Monday officially but I'd rather buy her a coffee on her first day than have a fire on day two. I'll send her a slack ahead of time so she has context. Actually — I'll send her the log file from today's run. If she has thirty minutes between feeding the baby and starting work next week, she can probably see in the logs what I haven't seen yet. Carla has a way of looking at logs that I don't. I keep telling Andrés that working with Carla is half the value of working at Brújula and he's about to find out why.

Camila came into the kitchen at 11pm with two glasses of water and asked what I was working on. I tried to explain at a high level — 'the service is slow under burst even though the CPU is not busy, which means the runtime itself is starved' — and she said 'so what's it doing?' and I said 'queueing.' She said 'is that like when you're queuing in your head and can't sleep' and I laughed because she has been making fun of my insomnia framing for weeks but the analogy actually fits — queueing-without-progress, work piling up faster than the system can drain it. She's been calling my insomnia my 'cognitive backlog' for months. Tonight the joke landed differently. The runtime and the brain do similar things. Both have a single executor that gets overwhelmed when one task takes too long and won't yield. Tomorrow I find the offender, replace it, and ship the fix. I wish my own runtime was that easy to debug. Going to bed now. Or trying.

One more quick thought before bed: I keep telling Andrés that runtime starvation is one of those bugs that is invisible until you're staring directly at it, then suddenly seems obvious. Like the duck-rabbit illusion. From the wrong angle the dashboard says 'CPU is fine, network is fine, database is fine, why is it slow?' and you can stare at that for hours without seeing the answer. The right angle is asking 'what is the runtime doing?' and the answer becomes immediately visible the moment the question is correctly framed. Asking the right question is 80% of debugging. The mechanical work of tracing logs, reading code, instrumenting the runtime — that's the easy 20%. The hard 80% is forming the hypothesis that focuses the search. I think this is what Carla is going to teach Andrés over the next quarter without saying it out loud — how to form the hypothesis. The mechanical work he can already do. The hypothesis-formation is the senior skill, and you only learn it by being wrong enough times to start recognizing the patterns of when you're being wrong. I'm still learning it myself. The runtime starvation diagnosis took me longer than it should have because I kept assuming the bug was in the database layer or the connection pool — places I'd seen bugs before — and not in the runtime, which I'd never had to debug at this depth. New territory. New question to ask. The next time this kind of bug shows up I'll spot it in an hour instead of six. That's the value of the experience. The next bug will pay back the cost of this one.

Profe announced today that he wants me and two others doing extra Saturday classes from now until the test. That's on top of Mon/Wed/Fri evenings. So four days a week of training, plus Sunday yoga because Camila made me promise back when I was being a baby about it and I can't renege on the promise without provoking a discussion I don't have the energy for. Four-day-week is not insane on paper but with the rust migration heating up and the chess board on the bench at home and a body that's already at 50% on the back, it's going to be tight. Recovery time becomes the bottleneck.

Pablo's one of the others, thank god, otherwise the Saturday sessions would just be me eating shins from strangers I don't know and don't have rapport with. The third is a guy named Iván I don't know super well — taller than me, longer reach, lefty stance, going to be annoying to spar with because lefty stance changes all the angles. He's been at brown for two years, retesting from a previous black belt attempt that didn't go his way. So he's hungry. He's going to come at the Saturday classes hard.

Profe says these Saturdays are 'the cruel ones.' His exact phrasing. He smiled when he said cruel, which is the smile he does when he is about to enjoy your suffering more than is strictly professional. Sparring rounds with no break, full-contact bag work, then forms drilling until your legs shake and the other students who are NOT testing get to watch us suffer for educational purposes. The Saturday class is also when the noon-class students show up, so we'll have a bigger audience than the Tuesday/Thursday evening crowd. More pressure. Profe likes pressure. Profe believes pressure is a teacher.

Brown belt was a year ago and I remember thinking that test was hard. The brown belt test was structured around endurance and technique — long rounds, less variety, can-you-keep-going. Word on the mat is the black belt prep eats your soul because it's not just endurance, it's that you have to be sharp on top of being tired. Everything you can do at full energy you also need to do on dead legs, with a foggy brain, after three sparring rounds. Pablo's already complaining about gas usage, which is funny because he runs ten kilometers twice a week and is in better cardio shape than I am. I'm just trying to keep the back from giving out before the test. Drinking water. Sleeping when I can. Buying more electrolyte mix. I'll do what Profe says because Profe is right about training even when he's wrong about other things, like which mezcals are best (he's wrong about Vago Espadín — it's good).

I'm telling Camila about the Saturday addition tonight at dinner. She's going to make a face and then say something supportive. We've gotten good at this dance over five years — me adding things to my plate, her absorbing the schedule shift with grace and one well-placed comment about how I should be sleeping more. The five-year version of this conversation is shorter than the early-relationship version because we both know the script. The key innovation in our version is that she doesn't try to talk me out of the thing — she just asks what she can take off my plate during the high-load weeks. Last time it was groceries. This time it'll probably be groceries again. I'll send her a Rappi link with the order pre-built and she'll handle the rest.

I asked Pablo on the drive home today whether he ever considers quitting before the test. He said no. I said I do, sometimes, in low moments — usually around 11pm on a Wednesday when the back is hurting and I have a 6am meeting the next day and the prospect of two more hours at the gym feels insurmountable. He said his version is different — he doesn't consider quitting kickboxing, but he considers postponing the test sometimes, telling Profe he needs another six months. I told him postponing-while-staying is the same shape as quitting in terms of preserving the option. The thing that matters is showing up to the test on the date. He nodded. Same conclusion, different framing. Two more weeks. Showing up.

Found something. Running with a pared-down RUST_LOG=trace and grepping for `tokio::runtime::worker` in the output, I can see lines like:

```
TRACE tokio::runtime::worker: parking worker=0
TRACE tokio::runtime::worker: unparking worker=0
TRACE tokio::runtime::scheduler::current_thread: scheduling task tid=task_392841
TRACE tokio::runtime::scheduler::current_thread: blocking on task tid=task_392841 duration=412ms
TRACE tokio::runtime::scheduler::current_thread: blocking on task tid=task_393019 duration=388ms
TRACE tokio::runtime::scheduler::current_thread: blocking on task tid=task_393204 duration=445ms
TRACE tokio::runtime::scheduler::current_thread: blocking on task tid=task_393388 duration=421ms
TRACE tokio::runtime::scheduler::current_thread: blocking on task tid=task_393571 duration=390ms
```

That 412ms duration is the smoking gun. A single task held the executor for 412 milliseconds of wall-clock time without yielding back to the runtime. And the next four are over 350ms each, in rapid succession during the burst window. Under 1200 RPS, even a single 400ms blocking task means a queue of nearly 500 incoming tasks all waiting behind it. And we're getting these 400ms blocking events repeatedly during the burst. So the queue isn't draining — it's compounding.

Now I just need to find which task it is. The trace tid maps to a span if I get the trace ID into the log line, which I haven't done yet because the default span propagation doesn't include task names. Going to add `tokio::task::Builder::new().name(...)` everywhere we spawn so the trace lines tell me what blocked. That's a 30-minute refactor for the dozen-or-so spawn sites we have. Worth it for the diagnostic clarity. Will report back when I have the culprit named.

Separately — the duration values themselves are interesting. They're tightly clustered around 400ms. Not a power-law distribution, not a long tail. Suggests the offending workload has a deterministic cost rather than a tail-amplifying random one. Which means once I find it, the fix should be fully deterministic too. Either pulling it out of the async path entirely with spawn_blocking, or chunking it with .yield_now() between iterations to give the runtime a chance to schedule other work in between.

Camila asked me what I'm working on at dinner and I tried to explain task starvation at the level a non-engineer can engage with. The metaphor I landed on: imagine a kitchen with one chef. Orders come in from the dining room. The chef can take an order, start cooking, then while the dish is in the oven they can take another order, start prepping that one. Multitasking by interleaving. But if one specific dish requires the chef to stand at the stove and stir constantly for 7 minutes — no oven, no walking away — every other order in the kitchen waits until the stirring is done. The dining room doesn't see one slow dish. The dining room sees every dish from that 7-minute window come out late, all at once, in a clump. That's task starvation. The chef is the runtime. The 7-minute stirring task is the offending blocking work. The clump-of-late-dishes is the post-burst latency tail. She got it immediately and said 'so the answer is hire another chef?' Yes. Add another chef (multi-thread runtime). But also figure out which dish is the stirring problem and consider sending it to a different kitchen entirely (spawn_blocking). Two complementary fixes. She nodded. I'm always impressed when she gets technical analogies on the first try.

What I want Andrés to take from watching me trace through this: the diagnostic flow that gets you from 'something is wrong' to 'here's the bug.' It's never linear. You form a hypothesis, you test it, the test rules it out, you form a new hypothesis. The trick is to track which hypotheses you've ruled out and which you haven't, so you don't end up looping back to the same wrong answer at midnight in a fog of fatigue. I keep a running list in a scratch text file when I'm in deep debug mode. Andrés asked to see the file. I sent it to him.

3:14am. Wide awake. Brain is doing the thing where it's running through the rust trace logs in a loop, like a stuck future polling itself with no progress. I went to bed at 11:30. Five and a half hours of sleep coming if I fall back asleep right now, which I won't. New thing I'm noticing — this is the third night this week I've woken up between 3 and 3:30am sharp, and once I'm up, I'm UP. Not drowsy-up. Fully alert, mind racing, heart slightly elevated. Anyone else have this where the body just decides 3am is the new wake time?

Alright. Caffeine. Yerba mate cup #2 of the morning, brewed strong because I've had four hours of sleep. While I was lying awake at 3am I had a hypothesis and I think I just confirmed it on the test box. Going to type this out because I'm half thinking through it as I write, and writing it out helps me catch the parts I haven't actually verified.

The 412ms task is our route geometry decoder. We pull the route shape as a PostGIS geography blob — lon/lat polylines stored compactly in the database in WKB binary format. We decode it to a flattened `Vec<(f64, f64)>` for the optimizer pipeline, and then run a Douglas-Peucker simplification on it to reduce point count before passing it to the dispatch heuristic. Standard stuff for any geo service. The simplification is pure CPU. It's a tight recursive loop over potentially thousands of points, computing perpendicular distances from each point to a chord between endpoints, splitting the segment at the maximum-distance point if it exceeds a threshold, recursing on both halves. And — this is the key bit — the recursion is *not* awaiting anything inside that loop. It's pure sync code, sandwiched between async function boundaries.

So when this handler hits, the current_thread executor sits on it until simplification completes and the surrounding future yields naturally at the next await point (which is the dispatch heuristic's first DB call, after simplification finishes). With 18 waypoints expanded into multi-segment shapes, each shape can be 3-10k points depending on the route's geometric complexity. At 1200 RPS, you're looking at... yeah. Disaster. The simplification function is profile-friendly — I just ran cargo flamegraph against a synthetic call and it sits at the top of the profile chart for the duration. Easy to spot once you know to look for it. Should have caught it earlier. We had load tests but they were at lower RPS where the runtime had enough idle time between calls to hide the issue.

The naive fix: spawn_blocking. Move the simplification into the blocking thread pool, await its completion. That keeps the async executor free to handle other requests while the CPU-bound simplification happens on a dedicated OS thread. Drop-in swap, basically — the function signature stays sync, you just wrap the call site in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || simplify(points)).await`. Tokio's blocking pool is sized at 512 threads by default, so we have headroom for hundreds of concurrent simplifications without saturating the pool.

I just did it on a feature branch and re-ran the burst test on staging-light. p99 went from 4200ms to 180ms. Healthz stays clean throughout the burst, never climbs above 2ms. Done. Like, technically done — the immediate fire is out. The fix is small enough that it's testable in isolation, easy to review, low blast radius.

But. I want to be principled about this. spawn_blocking is fine when you have one CPU-bound thing. We will have more — there's a clustering pass downstream that'll have the same property under load (it's a k-means variant with fixed iteration count, also pure CPU), and Andrés is writing a graph traversal for late-stage rerouting that'll probably also block. If I sprinkle spawn_blocking everywhere I'm essentially admitting we want a multi-thread runtime, and I should just commit to that as the architectural answer. Switch the runtime flavor at startup, give it 4 worker threads on our 8-core boxes (leaving slack for spawn_blocking pool, GC, sidecars), and then the default `tokio::main(flavor = "multi_thread", worker_threads = 4)` handles the parallelism for me without having to manually annotate every CPU-bound block. Each worker thread can independently schedule async tasks, so a single CPU-bound handler on one worker doesn't starve the others.

Do I do (a) the surgical spawn_blocking fix or (b) the runtime-flavor change? My instinct is (b) for the long term, but rolling out in two steps — first ship spawn_blocking surgically since it's testable in isolation and very low-blast-radius, then swap the runtime flavor in a separate PR with its own load test. Two-step gives me clearer revert if multi-thread surfaces some surprise (e.g. a `!Send` future that compiles fine on current_thread but breaks on multi_thread, or a thread-local guard that misbehaves across threads, or a global state assumption that depends on single-thread serialization).

The blocking pool is the right abstraction for 'I need to do CPU work, please do it without blocking my async progress.'

Did it. Both fixes are in. PR #847 is the spawn_blocking surgical, merged this morning at 9:32 after Andrés gave it the lgtm. PR #851 is the multi-thread runtime swap, in review with Andrés as we speak. Going to write this up while it's fresh because I want to remember the exact failure modes for the future, and because the wiki post I owe Roberto needs source material.

The runtime swap was almost a one-liner at the call site:

```rust
// before
#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
    routeopt::server::run().await
}

// after
#[tokio::main(flavor = "multi_thread", worker_threads = 4)]
async fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
    routeopt::server::run().await
}
```

That's it for the actual switch. I added `worker_threads = 4` explicitly because the default is `num_cpus()` which on our k8s pods reports the host CPU count not the pod limit — the host has 16 cores, our pod gets 2 reserved with a 4-core limit. If we let tokio see 16, it would spin up 16 worker threads and we'd be context-switching heavily inside our 2-4 core budget. Hard-coding to 4 with our 2 CPU pod request and 4 CPU pod limit gives us deterministic behavior that matches the box's actual CPU capacity, leaves headroom for spawn_blocking threads to share cores, and avoids the kubernetes-tokio-mismatch trap. I do NOT want to fight that battle in production at 3am six months from now after we forgot how the runtime sees the world.

The scary part was the compiler failures. As I suspected, going from current_thread to multi_thread surfaced a handful of `!Send` issues. Five places, not three like I'd estimated:

1. Three places where we held an `Rc<RouteCache>` across an await — those needed to become `Arc<RouteCache>`. Mechanical change. The hot path doesn't care about the difference between Rc and Arc; the atomic refcount overhead is fractions of a nanosecond. Done in five minutes.

2. One place where a `tokio::sync::Mutex<RoutingTable>` was held across a spawn — that one was actually correct because tokio Mutex is Send-aware, but the borrow checker was angry because of how the future was structured (the lock guard was held in scope when we entered the spawn, even though the future inside the spawn didn't reference it). I refactored to release the lock before the spawn, which I should have been doing anyway as good async hygiene — never hold an async mutex across a spawn unless you really mean to share the lock with the spawned task.

3. One genuinely subtle one: we had a thread-local `tracing::span::EnteredSpan` guard held across an await in a middleware. Single-threaded runtime, no problem because the future never moves between threads, the span enter/exit happens on the same OS thread. Multi-threaded — the span enter/exit could end up on different threads if the future is moved between workers, and tracing's thread-local scoped storage breaks (or worse, silently misattributes spans, which is the worst kind of bug — wrong observability). Switched to `tracing::Instrument::instrument(future, span)` which is the proper way and I should have been doing from day one. The `tracing` docs literally warn against the `EnteredSpan` pattern in async code. I missed it. Andrés found it during review which is — annoying that I missed it but I'm glad he found it. Junior engineer catches senior engineer in subtle bug. Good for him.

I want to also write up the multi_thread switch decision because I'll need to explain it to the broader engineering org and I don't want to write it cold tomorrow morning. Three reasons we're switching. First, we've already proven that one specific handler in our service does 400ms of CPU-bound work per call, and even after we wrap that work in spawn_blocking, we'll always have other handlers that do shorter but still-blocking work that benefit from a multi-thread runtime. Second, our service traffic is heterogeneous — different endpoints have different CPU profiles — and a multi-thread runtime is more resilient to a sudden mix shift than a current_thread one. If we suddenly get a flood of requests for a CPU-heavy endpoint, multi-thread spreads the work; current_thread queues it all up. Third, the cost of multi-thread is essentially zero for our throughput level — at 600-1200 RPS we're nowhere near the contention threshold where multi-thread overhead starts to matter, which is more like 50k+ RPS on more complex services.

The one cost is that multi_thread requires Send + 'static on all spawned futures, which means we can't hold non-Send types across .await boundaries. We have a couple of places in our codebase that use Rc<T> instead of Arc<T> for performance reasons — Rc is single-threaded, so it's faster than Arc but doesn't satisfy Send. Switching those to Arc costs us a few nanoseconds per refcount but removes the Send violation. Carla has already audited the codebase and found 14 sites that need updating. Most are trivial Rc → Arc swaps. Two are more involved — places where we hold a !Send database transaction across an await — and those will need refactoring to either commit before the await or use a different transaction pattern. Two days of work total, plus testing. The fix lands next week along with the multi_thread switch.

Long-term I'm starting to think we should also explore moving some of the heaviest CPU work to a separate Rust binary that runs as a sidecar service. Geometry decode, route optimization scoring, distance-matrix computation — these are all pure-CPU workloads with no IO, and they'd benefit from running in a process with a different runtime configuration than our request-handling main service. The idea would be: main service handles the request, hands off the CPU work over a local unix socket to the sidecar, the sidecar does the work and returns the result, main service builds the response. That separation would mean the request-handling runtime never has to deal with CPU-heavy tasks at all. spawn_blocking is a good band-aid but a separate process is the architecturally cleaner answer for this kind of mixed workload. Cost is operational complexity — two binaries to deploy instead of one, two sets of metrics, two health checks. We'd need to be confident the operational cost is worth the architectural benefit. For now, spawn_blocking is fine. The sidecar is something I'm filing away as a Q3 or Q4 architectural review topic. Carla agrees this is worth thinking about but not worth doing right now. Right now we have a migration to ship and a stable production pattern is more important than a theoretically optimal one. The good is the enemy of the perfect, in this case, and we're going with the good for now.

Title: When current_thread bites you, and how multi_thread fixes it (RouteOpt postmortem-lite)

We were running RouteOpt's HTTP server on a current_thread Tokio runtime — the default flavor when you write `#[tokio::main]` without any qualifiers, on the version of tokio we have pinned in our root Cargo.toml. It is the sensible default for many small services. It became a problem for us as scale increased.

Symptoms emerged under burst load testing (1200 RPS, 90s duration, 6 cycles, 5-minute intervals between bursts):

- p99 spiked from 38ms to 4200ms during bursts (a 110x increase)
- p99 stayed elevated 20s after burst ended (queue draining post-burst)
- /healthz endpoint, which returns immediately with no logic, also slowed dramatically during bursts (climbing into hundreds of ms — at peak, we saw healthz at 280ms, which is absurd for an endpoint that does literally nothing but return 200)
- CPU usage on the box was ~18% during the spikes — counterintuitive
- Memory was flat throughout — this was not a leak
- No external dependency was misbehaving — Postgres, Redis, network all clean

The healthz signal is the giveaway. If a trivial endpoint slows down at the same time as a complex endpoint, the runtime itself is starved, not your business logic. If only the complex endpoint slowed down, you'd suspect a CPU-bound bug in that handler, or a downstream dependency that's being blocked by a specific request shape, or a database query plan that goes pathological under specific inputs. The fact that HEALTHZ slowed down means the runtime literally couldn't schedule its handler in time, regardless of what the handler does. Healthz is your canary for runtime health.

Root cause: a CPU-bound bit of work in a request handler (Douglas-Peucker simplification on route geometry, ~3-10k points per call, recursive distance computation, no async yields) that did not yield to the runtime. On a single-threaded executor this monopolizes the runtime for hundreds of milliseconds per call — we measured 400ms+ blocking events from a single task in trace logs, repeatedly during bursts. Under burst load, all other tasks (including healthz) queue behind it, and the queue compounds because 400ms at 1200 RPS is 480 incoming tasks per blocking event.

Fix in two steps:

1. Wrap CPU-bound work in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` (immediate surgical fix, low-risk, testable in isolation, easy revert). This moves the CPU work to a dedicated OS thread pool and frees the async executor to handle other requests during the simplification.

2. Switch runtime to multi_thread with explicit worker_threads count (long-term architectural fix). This handles future CPU-bound code automatically by giving the runtime multiple cores to schedule across, and makes the system more resilient to single-handler stalls.

We did both, in that order, in separate PRs, with a full burst load test between each. Each PR was reviewed independently. The two-step rollout was deliberate — single PRs for revert clarity if either change surfaced unexpected issues.

Gotchas of the multi_thread switch (these surprised me):

- `Rc<T>` held across awaits will not compile under multi_thread (because futures may move between threads, and Rc is not Send). Change to `Arc<T>`. Mechanical fix, fractions of a nanosecond overhead per refcount op.
- `tokio::sync::Mutex` held across awaits is fine, but holding across a `spawn` may complain depending on guard scoping. Release before spawning. Async-mutex hygiene, and a good practice anyway because holding async locks across spawns is usually a sign of unclear ownership.
- Thread-local `tracing::span::EnteredSpan` guards held across awaits are a logic bug, even if they compiled before — use `tracing::Instrument::instrument(future, span)` instead. The tracing docs warn against this pattern but it's easy to miss because it compiles fine on current_thread and silently misattributes spans on multi_thread.
- `worker_threads` defaults to `num_cpus()` which on Kubernetes does NOT respect cgroup CPU limits — pin it explicitly to your pod CPU request or limit. We've seen this same trap in Java services with similar root cause; it's a generic 'runtimes don't read cgroups' problem.
- Watch for non-Send futures introduced by external crates (rare but possible, especially in older or niche crates); pin crate versions and test before upgrade.

What to monitor after the switch:

- `runtime.tasks.alive` from tokio-metrics — sanity-check no leak; should be flat over time
- spawn_blocking pool saturation (`runtime.blocking.threads.active`) — if you go above 256 active blocking tasks regularly, you're near the default 512 cap and should reconsider the workload
- Healthz latency under load — your canary for runtime starvation; alert if it ever exceeds 10ms
- Per-worker-thread CPU utilization — if one worker is consistently maxed and others are idle, you have a hot-task problem and should investigate

When NOT to multi_thread:

- Trivial CRUD services with no CPU-bound code can safely stay on current_thread; the overhead of multi_thread (work-stealing coordination, atomic refcounts) is small but real
- Per-request memory or context that you legitimately want isolated to a single thread (rare, usually cache-coherency micro-optimizations in very specific high-perf domains)
- Services where the entire workload fits comfortably on a single core and going wider would just add overhead without throughput improvement

Know your service's CPU profile before picking flavor. If your service has any work that is genuinely CPU-bound and not amenable to spawn_blocking — go multi_thread from day one. If you're not sure, multi_thread. If you're optimizing for the 95th-percentile case, multi_thread.

---

That's the draft. Honest question — am I being too prescriptive on 'go multi_thread from day one'? Some of our smaller services do almost nothing CPU and are fine on current_thread. I don't want this read as cargo-culting. Maybe I rephrase the closing as 'consider multi_thread as the default and current_thread as the optimization'? That feels more honest. Current_thread does have real benefits — slightly lower overhead, simpler debugging, deterministic ordering. But those benefits only matter for specific workloads, and my read is that most of our services don't fit that bucket. The notification service, for example, definitely should be multi_thread because it'll be doing template rendering and that's CPU-bound. The driver-app backend is more questionable — it's mostly CRUD with some auth checks. Maybe a worked-example section: 'Here's RouteOpt (multi_thread). Here's notifications (multi_thread). Here's a hypothetical thin proxy service (current_thread is fine).' Yeah, going to add that.

The other thing the load test today taught us is the importance of warm-up time on the multi_thread runtime. The first burst after pod startup had slightly worse p99 than subsequent bursts — about 250ms p99 vs 180ms after warm-up. The Tokio runtime takes a few seconds to spin up its worker threads to handle the first wave of work, and the thread pool's behavior is slightly different in the first 30 seconds of operation. We need to add a startup warm-up phase to the readiness check that fires synthetic traffic against the new pod before it's marked ready and added to the load balancer rotation. Otherwise we'll see latency spikes during deploys that correlate exactly with new-pod readiness signals.

Carla came up with the warm-up implementation: we add a startup hook that fires 100 synthetic dispatch requests against the local pod at /v2/dispatch with a special X-Warmup header that the service treats as a no-op (returns 200, doesn't actually do dispatch work, but exercises the runtime and the connection pool). The warmup completes in about 5 seconds. Pod is marked ready only after the warmup completes. The deploy timeline goes from 'pod ready in 8 seconds' to 'pod ready in 13 seconds.' Five extra seconds per pod is fine. The latency-spike-during-deploy that we'd otherwise see is much more expensive than 5 extra seconds of deploy time.

Andrés asked at the end of the day if the runtime starvation thing is going to be in his next interview prep when he applies for senior positions someday. I told him yes — multi-thread vs current_thread is one of those distinctions that interviewers love to ask about because it separates engineers who've worked at scale from engineers who've only done toy projects. The follow-up question, which is the harder one, is 'when would you NOT use multi-thread?' The answer is when your service does so little CPU work that the thread coordination overhead exceeds the parallelism benefit, or when you have other constraints (like deterministic single-threaded serialization for a specific safety-critical computation) that require single-threaded execution. He wrote it in his notebook. He'll be ready for that interview question in two years when he's actually in those interviews.

Profe gave us the test sequence today, the formal version. It's a beast. Five sections, in order, no breaks except for water.

First: forms. Three forms, full sequence, in front of the whole gym. Kata-style, choreographed kicks and strikes against an imaginary opponent, executed at full speed and full power. The forms are the only part you can rehearse to perfection because there's no opponent and no variability — same sequence every time. So if you mess up the forms, that's purely on you, and Profe will know. He's been watching me drill these for two years.

Second: target practice. Eight specific kick combos on the heavy bag, full power, called by Profe in random order. Each combo is 3-5 strikes. He may make you restart if you mess one up — restart from combo 1, not from where you stopped. He may make you restart just to mess with you and see how you handle the frustration. Equanimity is part of the test.

Third: sparring. Three rounds against three different partners — one your size, one bigger, one Profe himself. Three minutes each round, thirty seconds rest between rounds. The Profe round is the hardest because he reads everything you start to throw and counter-times it before it lands. The goal isn't to beat Profe, the goal is to demonstrate composure and skill under pressure from a much better fighter.

Fourth: board breaks. Three boards, three different strikes — one with elbow, one with knee, one with side kick. The boards are pine, scored on one side along the grain. They will break if you hit them right. They will hurt if you don't.

Fifth: verbal. You have to answer questions about kickbox philosophy, history, and the lineage of Atlas Box itself in front of the gym. The verbal section is the surprise to me — I'd known there'd be a verbal but I hadn't realized how comprehensive it would be. I asked Profe what kind of questions and he just said 'questions you should know the answer to if you're a black belt.' Helpful. Pablo is taking the verbal more seriously than the physical, which is exactly Pablo — he's a mechanical engineer, of course he's making flashcards. He brought them to my apartment Sunday and we drilled for two hours. Camila walked through the kitchen at one point and said 'ustedes son tan ñoños' which is the most accurate observation she has ever made about us.

The verbal section is also where Profe's teaching philosophy comes through. He thinks if you can fight but you can't articulate why you fight, you're not a black belt yet — you're a fighter who happens to know forms. The verbal forces you to reason about why the discipline exists, where it came from, what it means to take on the responsibility of becoming a teacher even informally. He doesn't say 'teacher' explicitly during the test but the questions are designed to surface whether you've thought about this stuff or just accumulated reps without reflection. I've thought about it. Pablo's thought about it more than I have. We've had hours of conversations about lineage and discipline and what we owe to the students younger than us, sitting at Cardinal café over cortados while Mariana the barista pretends not to listen but absolutely listens. The verbal will be the easiest section if I can stay calm. Calm under interrogation has never been my natural state. We'll see.

Profe also said something subtle that I want to capture: he doesn't post the test sequence ahead of time except for the testees themselves. The other students don't know the format until they see it executed. The reason, he says, is that the unknown sequence is part of why the test is hard — you can't pre-rehearse the energy distribution across the five sections if you don't know how they're ordered. Pablo asked once if he tweaks the order between cohorts. Profe smiled. That smile means yes, he does, and it's calibrated to each cohort's specific weaknesses. So our cohort will get a different sequence than the cohort before us. Probably ordered to test our specific tendencies. Mine: gas out under back-to-back sparring. Pablo's: rush combos. Iván's: lose composure under verbal pressure. The test will be tailored. Profe has watched us for years.

Camila pushed me to download a CBT-I app — Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia, the structured program version. There are like four major ones in this space and I tried two over the last three weeks. Here's the honest review because someone is going to ask me about this in three months and I want a record.

App 1: CBT-i Coach (free, made by the US VA — the Veterans Affairs department, which I learned by reading the about page). Two weeks of use.

Clinical and dry. The UI looks like it was designed in 2015 and never updated, which I respect in a 'this is a tool, not a product' way. Lots of forms — daily sleep diary (asked at wake time), beliefs questionnaires (taken once at intake), pre-sleep routine checklists, mood ratings. The core technique is sleep restriction therapy, which is the thing where they tell you to deliberately spend LESS time in bed (like, 6 hours total) so you build sleep pressure across the day, then gradually increase the window as efficiency improves. The logic is sound — sleep is partly homeostatic, you build pressure during waking hours, more pressure means deeper and more consolidated sleep, deeper sleep should reduce mid-night wake events.

The execution destroyed me. The app calculated my sleep efficiency from the diary entries (time in bed vs time actually asleep, as estimated by my report) and decided my permitted sleep window was 11:30pm to 4:30am. Five hours total. For five days minimum. I felt like a zombie. Did I sleep more solidly during that 5-hour window? Yes, actually — fewer wake-ups, deeper REM by the diary's measure, the wakes I did have were shorter. Did I function during the day? Absolutely not. I almost rear-ended someone on Insurgentes Sur at 9am because my reaction time was shot. I made a regex error in a code review at work that Andrés caught and was too polite to mock me for. I was useless from 2pm onwards. After day five I bailed.

App 2: Sleepio (paid, ~$400/year, actually went through my employer's wellness benefit which I didn't know I had until Camila pushed me to look it up — Brújula's HR portal has a health benefits page I'd never opened). One week of use.

Prettier, gamified, has a virtual sleep coach character ('the Prof' — yes really, an animated white guy in a cardigan, slightly creepy mostly because his lips don't quite sync with the audio). Sessions are weekly, structured, more like a Coursera class than an app — you do a session, then practice for a week, then come back for the next session. The content overlaps heavily with CBT-i Coach but the framing is friendlier and the data viz is actually good. They show you sleep efficiency trends over time, correlate with your daily ratings, identify patterns. If I were a UX person I'd respect the design work.

Same restriction technique though, dressed up in nicer clothes. Same problem — I couldn't sustain the time-in-bed restriction with my actual life. I have kickboxing four nights a week, demanding job with on-call rotations, a girlfriend who I want to spend evenings with, a dog who needs morning walks regardless of how I slept. Telling me to be in bed by 12 and out at 5 sharp doesn't survive contact with reality. I lasted one week before I started lying to the app about my actual bedtime to avoid the restriction calculation pulling tighter.

The stimulus control bit is more useful in theory than in practice — get out of bed if you can't sleep within 20 minutes, do something boring in dim light, return to bed only when sleepy. The idea is to break the bed-as-anxiety-spot conditioning. Bed should mean sleep, not 'lying awake worrying about a load test from yesterday.' But at 3am when the wake hits, the LAST thing my body wants is to leave the warm bed and stand in the cold kitchen reading a paper book under a small lamp. So I lie there, breaking the rule, and then I'm adding guilt about breaking the rule on top of the original insomnia. Net negative on stress.

What actually has helped, modestly: yerba mate cutoff at 2pm (down from 5pm), no kickboxing later than 7pm (had to renegotiate with Profe to skip the late Tuesday class), hot shower right before bed, dimming the apartment lights from 9pm via the smart bulbs Camila installed last year. Boring stuff. The CBT-I apps probably work great for someone with more lifestyle flexibility than I have right now. For someone who can spend two weeks reorganizing their entire schedule around sleep restriction without other obligations colliding. For me, in this season, with a black belt test and a rust migration and a chess board and a girlfriend and a dog — didn't stick. Both apps deleted off my phone last week. Camila looked relieved when I told her, which is not the response I expected — I thought she'd be disappointed that I quit. She said 'I'm glad you tried, but you weren't actually going to stick with it.' She knows me better than I know myself.

I know you can't prescribe me anything. Just venting. The plan is now: traditional good sleep hygiene without the structured-program overhead, and revisit if it's still bad in two months. Post-test. Post-rollout. When the calendar opens up.

One specific thing I want to record about the Sleepio sessions because it might come up if I describe this to Dr. Tovar at the appointment Camila wants me to book. Session 3 of Sleepio focused on cognitive restructuring around sleep beliefs. The Prof character (cardigan, lip-sync issues) walks you through a list of common dysfunctional sleep beliefs — 'I need 8 hours or I can't function,' 'if I don't sleep tonight, tomorrow is ruined,' 'I'm a poor sleeper, this is my identity now' — and asks you to identify which ones you hold and rate their conviction on a 0-100 scale. I rated 'I need 7+ hours or my decision-making degrades meaningfully' at 75. The Prof's response was that this belief is not actually well-supported by the evidence, that decision-making is robust to a wider range of sleep durations than we think, and that the belief itself is doing harm by amplifying the consequences of a bad night.

I disagree with the Prof on this specific point. I have specific anecdotal evidence — the regex error after 5 hours of sleep, the near-miss on Insurgentes Sur, the times I've shipped buggy code on bad-sleep weeks and fine code on good-sleep weeks — that points to a real performance degradation correlated with sleep quantity below 6 hours. The evidence in my life doesn't support the Prof's claim that the belief is unfounded. He's giving me a generic CBT script that may not apply to high-cognitive-load workdays. Or maybe my belief is partially true but the catastrophizing version of it is what's harmful — i.e., needing 7 hours is reasonable, but treating 5 hours as a 'ruined day' instead of a 'suboptimal day with workarounds' is the actually-harmful framing. That's a softer version of the cognitive restructuring that I could agree with. The app didn't offer that nuance. It went straight from 'belief identified' to 'belief is wrong, here's the rebuttal.' Felt like being lectured by an algorithm.

Other thing I want to remember: the sleep diaries themselves were useful even when the apps weren't. Logging time-in-bed, time-asleep-by-estimate, wake count, mood-on-wake — that data is independently useful regardless of which app collected it. I now know that my actual time-asleep is consistently 75-80% of time-in-bed, which is below the CBT-I 'good' threshold of 85% and explains the persistent low-grade fatigue even on weeks where I'm in bed for 8 hours. The numbers were the value, not the algorithmic prescriptions on top. I'll keep a manual sleep log going forward — Camila bought me a small notebook for it that lives on the nightstand, paper and pencil version, no screen, with a column for hours, wake count, dream content if any, and a 1-5 mood rating in the morning. Lower-tech than the apps. Actually sustainable. Maybe that's the real conclusion: the apps were over-engineered for a problem that wanted a paper notebook and a doctor.

The other CBT-I claim I want to register a disagreement with: the idea that lying awake in bed is bad for sleep conditioning, and you should get up if you can't sleep within 20 minutes. The logic is that the bed should only be associated with sleep, not with frustration or wakefulness. So if you're not sleeping, you should leave the bed and do something boring elsewhere until you're sleepy. I get the logic. I disagree with the practical application for a couple of reasons. First, getting up at 3am to sit in a cold dark kitchen reading a paper book under a small lamp is, for me, not boring — it's stimulating, because the cold and the silence and the unusual hour create a distinct experience that my brain wakes up further to engage with. The conditioning that's supposed to weaken (bed = wakefulness) gets replaced by a different conditioning (3am = adventure), which I'd argue is worse. Second, the 20-minute timer is a hard threshold that doesn't fit how my body actually works. Some nights I lie awake for 25 minutes and then drift back to sleep effortlessly. The CBT-I rule would have me get up at minute 21 and miss the natural drift. Third, the implicit framing is that a wakeful 30 minutes in bed is wasted time. I disagree. I do some of my best mental synthesis lying still in the dark, half-asleep, where the brain runs in a different mode than fully awake. Some of my best ideas have come at 3am while ostensibly trying to sleep, including the architecture sketch for the rust migration which I worked out in my head one night in month 3 of prep before writing it down at 5am. Productive insomnia is real. I'm not going to optimize it away just because a generic CBT-I script says I should.

Carla pushed her synthetic spawn_blocking saturation test today and we found a real thing — at 600+ concurrent CPU jobs, the spawn_blocking pool starts reusing threads, and the OS context switching overhead becomes meaningful enough to show up in latency. Latency degrades sub-linearly but it does degrade — about 8% p99 increase per additional 100 concurrent blocking tasks beyond 600. Realistic load is nowhere near that — we'd need 600 simultaneous geometry decodes and our peak is 200ish — but Carla wants us to add a hard cap and a metric so if we ever cross it we know.

Done deal. Capped at 256 explicitly via `tokio::runtime::Builder::new_multi_thread().max_blocking_threads(256)` in our custom runtime construction. Metric exposed via tokio-metrics as `runtime.blocking.threads.active`. Alert wired up in our prometheus rules to fire if we cross 200 sustained for 5 minutes — that gives us early warning before we'd actually hit the cap, so we have time to investigate why our blocking workload doubled before it becomes a saturation problem.

Andrés watched her debug it. She had me share my screen, walked him through her diagnostic flow — start with metrics dashboards, narrow with logs, confirm with strace on a running pod, validate with re-run after the fix. Five steps, twenty minutes from 'I think there's something here' to 'here's the patch.' Andrés said afterward 'I feel like I just watched a magic trick.' Same. Carla is, in her quiet way, the best rust engineer I've ever worked with — she's not flashy, she doesn't post on Mastodon about it, she just consistently produces correct work and finds bugs other people miss. Andrés is going to learn more from her in three months than he learned from me in the previous nine. That's fine. I'm here for it. Senior leveling up junior is the most valuable thing on the team right now and I'm glad it's not all on me anymore.

Specifically the diagnostic flow Carla walked Andrés through, because I want to remember this for when I'm coaching juniors more directly: (1) form a hypothesis from metrics — what panel showed weird? what does the shape of the weird suggest? (2) narrow with logs — find the events corresponding to the weird, look at structure, is it bursty, sustained, periodic? (3) confirm the hypothesis by reading code along the path — does the code path match the hypothesis or contradict it? (4) reproduce the issue under controlled conditions — synthetic test that triggers the metric pattern on demand, (5) ship a patch with a measurement so you can verify the fix and detect regressions. Five steps. Standard discipline. Most engineers skip 1 and 4 and get burned by partial diagnoses or untested fixes. Carla never skips. She'd been awake six hours, just back from four months off, and she ran the full sequence cold without reference notes. Andrés was sitting next to me watching it unfold and I could see in his face the moment when he realized that Carla had been operating at this level for years. The look was a mix of admiration and 'I have so much to learn.' Both warranted. I felt the same way the first time I saw her debug something three years ago at her old company before she joined us, when I was interviewing her and she fixed a bug in our take-home assignment in real time during the conversation.

Carla also pulled an old story out from her last job, where she worked on a similar service that hit a similar runtime starvation pattern — except they discovered it in production at 3am during a Black Friday surge, with paged engineers awake all night. She said our discovery via pre-prod load testing is the version of this experience that doesn't ruin anyone's life. The 3am-prod version teaches you the same lesson with much higher cost. I've been there before too — the postgres outage was my version. The memory regression we caught earlier in this migration was another version, smaller scale. You'd think we'd learn faster. We do learn but the universe keeps providing new failure modes faster than we can learn the old ones. Anyway. The catch this time was cheap.

3:08am. Brain on rust. Going to write code instead of staring at the ceiling because at least if I'm coding the wakefulness is productive. There's a follow-up ticket I've been meaning to clear from the rollout backlog. Camila is asleep. Nube is asleep on my feet, snoring lightly. The apartment is quiet except for La Polar's last drunk customer arguing with a taxi driver outside. Roma Norte at 3am has a specific atmosphere I have come to know intimately.

Since I'm awake at 3am again — 3am thoughts, take them with the appropriate amount of salt and the assumption that anything I say tonight I will partly disagree with by 9am tomorrow when the sun is up and the brain has reset to its more reasonable defaults.

I keep wondering if the insomnia is a symptom or a cause. Like, am I sleeping badly because I'm stressed about the rust migration, the black belt test, the chess board, the MIT essay, all of it? Or is it that I've optimized so much of my waking life that my body has nowhere to put the tension except into sleep? Camila's friend who's a psychiatrist said something at a dinner months ago that stuck — that for high-functioning people, anxiety often presents as somatic stuff, not as obvious worry. Sleep, gut, jaw, headaches, weird tingly extremities. My jaw is fine — Camila checked, she's been a teeth-grinder herself for years and knows the signs. My gut is fine — no IBS, no reflux, no weird food sensitivities. Sleep is the canary. The body has chosen sleep as the place to surface whatever it is.

What's interesting is what I was sleeping fine THROUGH. I was sleeping fine through the postgres outage last year — 4-hour prod incident, I was on call, wrote the post-mortem, and I slept seven hours that Sunday night after it resolved. I was sleeping fine through Beatriz's funeral in São Paulo. Through dad's bypass surgery in 2022 when we genuinely thought he might not make it. Through the worst weeks of those events. So it's not stress in any obvious correlated-with-bad-events way. The insomnia started right around when the rust migration entered the home stretch and the black belt prep ramped to four days a week. Both things I want, intensely. Things I've been working toward for over a year. Not stress in the bad sense — anticipation? Excitement that doesn't have anywhere to land in the daytime so it shows up at night?

I also notice I'm not bored. Pre-arc, I had bored evenings. I'd put on a record, lie on the couch, watch the ceiling fan, let my brain rest in idle. Now there's always a thing — chess board sanding, code review for Andrés, sparring rounds, an MIT essay forming in my head that I haven't started writing, a podcast about distributed systems in the background while I clean the kitchen, vinyl shopping research for the next purchase. The 'never bored' might be the problem. Idle time is when the body recovers from the neurological accumulation of the day. I've crowded out the idle time in pursuit of being maximally engaged with the things I care about, and the body is presenting me with the bill at 3am.

The most useful framing I've heard is from a podcast (of course I heard it on a podcast, that's where ideas come from now) — insomnia is your body saying 'I'm not done with the day.' Whatever I haven't processed before bed, my brain comes back for at 3am to finish processing because it's the first quiet space available with no incoming signal. The CBT-I people would call this rumination but I don't know if that's quite right. It's more like — open tabs in a browser. Tasks that started during the day and never got closed out. So the brain comes back to them at 3am because there's quiet space and nothing else competing for cycles, and it grinds through the queue of unfinished mental processes.

If that framing is right, the fix isn't sleep technique — it's day-time technique. Close more tabs during the day. Have actual decompression rituals between work and home. Process emotional stuff out loud rather than in my head, which means more conversations with Camila about non-logistical things, which means dinners that aren't us both on our laptops at the table. The CBT-I apps might be solving the wrong problem for me, which would explain why they didn't stick — they assume the problem is at bedtime and try to engineer bedtime, when actually the problem is upstream during the daytime and bedtime is just the symptom site.

Alternatively, the framing is wrong, and I'm just a 33-year-old man with a chemical imbalance who needs to talk to a doctor about it instead of writing 3am philosophical posts and pretending self-analysis is the same as treatment. That's also possible. Probably both, in different proportions. Going to drink water and try to close some tabs. Not the browser tabs. The mental ones. The browser ones I'll keep open because right now they include three rust forum threads about runtime behavior that are genuinely useful for tomorrow's work, and I'm not closing those for any psychiatrist or any podcast. Priorities.

Other thing I keep noticing at 3am: I have stopped reading for pleasure. Not in a 'I have no time' way — I have time, I just don't pick up books anymore. The last novel I finished cover-to-cover was almost a year ago, when Camila and I went to Tulum for a weekend and I read a Bolaño on the beach because there was nothing to do and no wifi. Before that, I read constantly, half a book a week minimum. Now my reading is almost entirely technical — rust forum posts, papers on distributed systems, woodworking blog entries about grain orientation, kickboxing technique articles. All instrumental. All pointed at things I'm trying to do better. None of it for the pleasure of reading itself.

That might be the same shape as the not-being-bored thing. I've optimized away the slack in my mental life, and one of the casualties is fiction, which exists outside instrumentality. Fiction doesn't make you better at anything specific. It does something more diffuse — gives the brain a different kind of input than the optimization-shaped inputs of the rest of the day, lets it process narrative and emotion at a leisurely pace, lets characters exist in your head without you needing to extract value from them. I've cut that out. I cut it out without noticing.

Camila reads constantly. She always has. She has a book in her bag at all times, on the nightstand, on the kitchen counter, in the car. She reads in transit on the metrobus to her firm. She reads at restaurants while waiting for me to arrive when I'm late, which I often am. She reads Clarice Lispector and Jorge Luis Borges and contemporary Mexican writers I should be reading more of like Fernanda Melchor and Valeria Luiselli. I've been quietly noticing for months that her mind feels more spacious than mine these days. Less optimized, more permeable, more open to surprise. I think the reading is part of why. It's not the only reason — Camila is a different kind of person from me, fundamentally — but the reading is at least adjacent to the difference.

I'm going to start reading fiction again. Not as another optimization target — that would be missing the entire point. Just as a thing I do, for thirty minutes a day, before bed if I can. Camila has a stack of books she's recommended to me over the past year that I have not opened. The MIT essay can wait. The rust forum threads can wait. I'm going to start with the Lispector that's been on my bedside table for four months unread. If the insomnia is the body saying 'we have unfinished tabs,' maybe the answer is partly 'open a different kind of tab.' One that's allowed to stay open for a while. One that doesn't need to close out tonight.

Anyway. 4:11am now. The horizon is gray-pink at the bottom edge of the window. Nube is sleeping under the bed where she goes when I'm restless because she has decided that the under-bed darkness is more peaceful than the bedroom. Camila is sleeping with one arm out from under the blanket which is her tell that she's deeply asleep and warm enough. I'm going to put the phone down. I'm going to lie still for fifteen minutes without trying to sleep, just lying. If I sleep, I sleep. If I don't, I get up at 5 and have a slow morning. Either is fine.

I'm also going to register, for honesty's sake, that the 3am philosophical posts are themselves a coping mechanism that I'm not sure is healthy. Writing through the wakefulness gives the brain something to do other than spiral. It produces text that looks like productive synthesis but might just be the rumination wearing a more articulate costume. Camila wakes up at 6:30 and finds me having written 1500 words at 3:42am and she's torn between being impressed (the writing is sometimes good) and being concerned (I should be sleeping). I'm torn the same way. Tonight I'm going to write less and lie still more. I'm setting a 200-word limit on this entry and I'm going to honor it.

Counting now: this entry is already past 200 words including the meta-commentary about the 200-word limit, which is itself the 3am brain doing the thing where it converts every constraint into a meta-puzzle to be cleverly handled instead of obeyed. Camila would call this 'why you don't sleep, mi amor.' She'd be right. Phone going down. Eyes closing. Going to count breaths. 4 in, 4 hold, 4 out, 4 hold. We'll see.

The other 3am realization, less philosophical and more practical: I've been trying to optimize sleep with the same intensity I optimize everything else, and the optimization itself might be making the problem worse. Sleep is one of the few human functions that resists optimization because it requires letting go, and letting go is the opposite of trying. The harder I try to fix the sleep, the more I'm in the wrong mode for actually sleeping. Camila has been saying this for weeks and I've been receiving it as a soft suggestion rather than as the actual diagnosis. She's right. Going to read fiction tonight, drink chamomile, no melatonin, no app, no diary entry, no plan. Just lie down and see what happens. Engineering brain off.

Pablo and I started doing the verbal prep on Sunday afternoons over coffee at Cardinal. Going through the history of muay thai, the import to American kickboxing in the 70s and 80s, the Dutch styles that emerged from Mejiro Gym in Amsterdam in the 80s and 90s, the modern hybrid systems that mix all three lineages. Profe gave us a reading list which is basically four photocopied pages stapled together (the original copies are yellowing — Profe's been using the same handout for 20 years) and a Bruce Lee quote book his abuelo gave him in 1972 that he keeps in a plastic sleeve in his office at the gym.

We're memorizing the lineage of Atlas Box itself — Profe trained under a guy named Benjamín 'Benji' Solís in Tijuana in the late 80s, Benji trained under one of the Sityodtong fighters who came to LA in the early 80s and split off to form a small school in Otay Mesa, that fighter trained under the original Sityodtong line in Bangkok which traces back to Kru Yodtong Senanan in the mid-twentieth century. There will be a question about the lineage. There's always a question about the lineage. Profe likes to remind us that knowing where you come from is half of knowing where you're going, and at black belt we should be able to recite the names like family.

Pablo made flashcards. Of course Pablo made flashcards. He's a mechanical engineer, he's incapable of approaching any learning problem without a structured method. He's also made me a little study guide with bullet points organized by topic and historical period, which is both helpful and slightly insulting because he laid it out like I'm in fourth grade. I told him so. He said 'pero está funcionando, no?' He's right. It's working. I now know names of Dutch kickboxers from the 80s I'd never heard of two weeks ago — Rob Kaman, Ramon Dekkers, Ivan Hippolyte. Cardinal's barista Mariana started recognizing us as 'the kickboxing flashcard guys' which is a label I'm fine with.

The deeper thing the verbal prep is teaching me, which Profe probably planned, is that knowing the lineage forces you to take seriously the responsibility of being part of it. Saying 'I trained under Profe' is one thing. Saying 'I'm in the lineage that goes Sityodtong-Bangkok → LA Otay Mesa → Benji Solís Tijuana → Profe Hernández CDMX → Sebastián 2026' is a different thing entirely. The first is a fact about your training. The second is a position in a multi-generational continuity, with obligations attached.
