Mom forwarded the video to her old nursing buddies, women I've met maybe three times at quinceañeras and weddings throughout my childhood. They're sending heart emojis like I'm their own son. The video has done more PR for me in 12 hours than I did for myself in all of 2023. I'm sitting here a little embarrassed and a lot grateful. I texted Pablo 'hermano, te debo la vida' and he sent back '$200 USD por el video, pago en pesos.' He'll never let me actually pay him. The fee is a joke we'll keep using for the rest of our lives, the joke being one of those long-running jokes that gets funnier each time it's invoked because both parties know exactly what it means.

The thing nobody tells you about big life events is how much of the celebration happens in WhatsApp group chats afterward, asynchronously, with people you haven't seen in years suddenly showing up to celebrate. The pre-internet generation did this through letters and phone calls. We do it through emoji reactions and forwarded videos. Same energy, different channel. Mom is from a generation that wrote handwritten letters to congratulate births and graduations; my generation forwards Pablo's video and adds three crying emojis. The bandwidth of the reaction is lower per-message but the reach is bigger and the response time is faster. Different trade-offs. I'm not sure either is better — handwritten letters were more durable and more thoughtful, but they didn't reach Tía Marisol in the same six-hour window. The asynchronous reach of the WhatsApp version is what made the warmth feel widely distributed instead of locally concentrated. Both modalities have their place. Mom would still prefer letters.

Even Mateo's wedding-planning chaos — which has been a slow-motion train wreck of texts about seating charts and DJ negotiations — paused for the test. He sent the family group a 'no wedding talk this weekend, just Sebastián talk' and Sofía co-signed it. They've been in deep wedding logistics for three months. Pausing it for two days was not nothing. I appreciated it more than I told them. Family members who can pause their own thing to honor someone else's is a sign of the family being healthier than I sometimes give us credit for.

Mateo and Sofía stayed an extra two days after the test, which I hadn't planned for but am very glad about in retrospect. Mateo and I haven't spent this much continuous time together since I moved to CDMX two years ago, and most of our communication since then has been WhatsApp messages and the occasional phone call when something major happens. Two full days of being in the same physical space is a different mode entirely.

We took La Torta out to Tepoztlán on Saturday for a day trip — Sofía wanted to see the pyramid, mom and dad joined, Camila stayed home with Nube and a deadline at her firm she'd been pushing all week. The four of us drove out — Mateo riding shotgun because he claimed shotgun before mom could (she let him, knowing she'd lose), mom and Sofía in the back row chatting about wedding planning the whole way. Did the climb up the Tepozteco trail (mom and dad waited at the base because dad's heart and the altitude don't agree, and dad was good-humored about it, made a joke about being the basecamp manager). Ate quesadillas at the market afterward, took photos with the cliff in the background that mom is going to print and frame because that's what she does with photos.

Mateo and I ended up sitting on a stone wall behind the market while Sofía and mom and dad were in a tianguis stall buying handicrafts that mom would later try to give me as housewarming gifts even though we've lived in this apartment for two years. The wall faced the valley. The wind came up from below carrying the sound of the market and a ranchera from a speaker at one of the food stalls.

He asked me directly if I'm thinking about the engagement. Camila and I, the engagement, the next step. I said yes, sometimes. More than I admit out loud, even to Camila, even in the couples therapy sessions we did briefly back in month 7 that paused after three. He asked if Camila wants to. I said she's mentioned it more times than I've responded to her mentions, which is itself a kind of answer that we both know counts as a non-answer. He nodded.

Then he said something I didn't expect and didn't want to hear and needed to hear — 'no te apures por mi boda, hermano.' That if Camila and I were going to do something, we should do it because we wanted to, not because Mateo's October wedding in Tijuana was creating an artificial deadline that I had been treating as an unspoken pressure for months. He's two years younger than me and he was telling me, his older brother, to take my own time about the most personal thing in my life. The reversal felt right somehow. He's grown up faster than I've noticed.

We sat there for a while without talking. The wind came up the slope from the valley. A bird I couldn't name called from somewhere in the trees behind us, three notes descending. Then he said 'pero igual, sí casarte primero estaría chido.' Older-brother prerogative restored, which is also Mateo making sure he hasn't tilted the dynamic too far toward sentiment. We laughed.

Sofía is good for him. I've watched them this weekend more carefully than I usually do. She's calm in the way Mateo isn't — he's high-energy, talks fast, jumps from topic to topic mid-sentence, makes connections that are interesting but sometimes leave the listener three topics behind — and she takes his energy and gives it shape, slows it down without dulling it. She finishes his sentences when he loses the thread, in a way that doesn't feel like correction but like translation. They're getting married in October in Tijuana, three months from now. I'm best man — I haven't actually been formally asked but he keeps saying things like 'cuando hagas tu speech como padrino' and I'm not going to correct him. He'll get to it eventually. The fact that he hasn't formally asked me yet is a Mateo thing where he's nervous I'll say no, even though we both know I'll say yes immediately.

We drove back to CDMX in silence for the last hour, mom asleep in the back seat with her head against the window, dad reading on his phone, Sofía and Mateo holding hands across the middle row armrest. Mateo and I have always been quiet at the end of long days. It's a thing we got from dad, who was quiet at the end of his teaching days when we were kids and who taught us by example that some kinds of tiredness are best honored with silence rather than speech. The quiet between us isn't tense — it's the comfortable kind, where you've said enough already and the rest doesn't need to be said. La Torta hummed along the highway. The sun went down behind us as we came into the valley. We hit traffic at Cuernavaca like always. Mom woke up briefly, looked out the window, said 'qué bonito atardecer,' and went back to sleep.

That night when we got home and dad was settled with mom watching TV in the guest room, I went out on the apartment's small balcony with a glass of mezcal and the bombilla cup full of yerba mate (mate first, then mezcal — I don't mix them obviously). The balcony faces an interior courtyard, no view, just other apartment windows and a couple of jacaranda trees in the courtyard below that Mama Lupita would have liked. I sat there for maybe an hour thinking about Mateo's question about the engagement. Camila and I have been together for five years. We've talked about marriage in low-stakes ways — passing comments, jokes, what kind of wedding we'd want. We've never had the actual conversation about timing or commitment. The not-having-the-conversation has itself become a thing.

Mateo's right that I shouldn't make the decision based on his wedding. But Mateo's wrong if he thinks his October wedding hasn't been quietly working on me for months. Watching him plan it. Watching him commit to it. Watching him become a person who makes that kind of commitment. There's a thing where younger siblings make decisions and the older sibling has to either follow or feel slightly behind, and that thing is operating on me whether I want it to or not. I don't think it's bad. I think it's a useful pressure that's surfacing my actual readiness rather than constructing an artificial one. If Mateo's wedding is making me ready, that means I'm ready, just with Mateo as the catalyst. The catalyst doesn't have to be the cause. It can just be the trigger that surfaces something already there.

I texted Camila from the balcony — she was asleep on the couch — asking if she wanted to walk Nube together at 6:30am tomorrow before the family wakes up. She didn't see it until morning. She said yes. We walked. We didn't talk about engagement. But we were walking together with the dog at a private hour, and I had the thought again that walking the dog together at 6:30am in a quiet city is one of those small recurring moments that makes a relationship into a partnership. We've done that walk maybe four hundred times together. Each one is a small repeat. Each one is its own evidence. The pattern of small evidences is more meaningful than any single conversation. The conversation will happen. Probably soon. Soon enough that Mateo will see it before October if my mom hasn't already strangled both of us by then with her not-pressuring questions. The decision is forming. The articulation is the last step. I'm going to stop forcing it and let it happen.

The day after Tepoztlán Mateo and Sofía took mom and dad out to a brunch place near Coyoacán while Camila and I had a few hours to ourselves at the apartment, the first quiet hours we'd had since the family arrived three days earlier. We didn't do anything big. We made coffee, sat on the couch, talked about her firm's project deadline and about my prod rollout next week and about whether the Saturday no-obligations thing was going to be one Saturday or every Saturday for a few weeks. She voted for several Saturdays. I agreed. We're going to renegotiate the home-life baseline now that the test is over and the rollout is winding into its slow phase. Less hyper-scheduling, more spaciousness. She's been carrying more of the household logistics for the past two months than is fair. I'm going to take back some of that load — grocery runs, dog vet, the building maintenance issue with the elevator that's been pending for weeks. Domestic equilibrium reset. We don't formalize these things often, but when we do, we always feel better afterward. The conversation lasted maybe twenty minutes and changed the texture of the rest of the week.

Mateo and Sofía left Sunday afternoon to drive back to Tijuana — they were going to stop in Querétaro overnight to visit Sofía's aunt — and the goodbye at the apartment had the weight that family goodbyes have when you don't see each other often. Mom and dad stayed another two days. Mom continued the kitchen reorganization to its bitter end and is now back in Guadalajara having achieved her actual goal of the trip, which was apparently to clean my apartment. The apartment hasn't looked this good since before we moved in. Camila is mildly horrified and mildly grateful — she's been trying to clean it on her own schedule for months and mom did in 48 hours what Camila and I together hadn't accomplished in a year.

Few days out from the test now and I'm trying to figure out what changed. The belt is in a glass display case Camila bought before the test — she'd ordered it online at week 5 of prep, not telling me, on the assumption I'd pass. She said later she wanted to be ready either way, but she ordered the case sized specifically for the black belt, not the brown belt I was wearing at the time. Vote of confidence I didn't know she was placing. The case sits on the entryway shelf next to my abuela's bombilla cup. Two objects from two different lineages — Mama Lupita's kindness from 2017 in Guadalajara before she died, and Profe's eight-year coaching arc culminating in primer dan. Both heavy in different ways. I find myself glancing at the shelf when I come home from work and when I leave the apartment in the morning. I never used to glance at that shelf. Now it's a small ritual. The ritual is forming on its own without my deciding it should.

I showed up to the gym on Monday like Profe said. Trained like a Monday. He didn't treat me different and I was glad — anything else would have felt off. Iván, who's retesting in three months, was already back at it, drilling forms in the corner before class started. He asked me to drill the third form sequence with him after class because he wants my spinning back fist transition. He'd watched it land clean during my test and wanted to know what I was doing differently from his version. I'd never been the person another student asked for technical help. Now I am, apparently. That's the only thing that feels different inside the gym, and it surprised me — Profe had warned us in the speech about becoming teachers whether we wanted to or not, and apparently it starts immediately, on Monday, before I've had time to think of myself as someone who teaches.

The video. Pablo's phone video. I've watched it eleven times now in three days, which is more than I've watched any video except maybe the Senna documentary in 2010. It's not vanity — or maybe it is, partially, and I should be okay with that — but the thing I keep watching is Profe's hands. He ties the belt and his hands don't shake at all and there's this weight to the gesture that the camera somehow caught even at a wide angle from the bench across the mat. Twenty-something black belts in his career and his hands tie each one the same way, with the same authority, the same care for the knot. There's something in that I don't quite have words for. I don't know if it'll matter to anyone but me ten years from now, when the video has aged poorly and the audio has compression artifacts and the image quality looks dated. But it'll matter to me, and that's enough. I'll watch it on my fortieth birthday probably. Maybe on my fiftieth. The video will mean different things at different ages.

The mood in the apartment has been weird since the family left on Sunday. Camila is happy for me but she's also tired — she had to do a lot of the party logistics with my family in town and her own architect deadlines stacking up at the firm at the same time, and the asymmetry of who-was-celebrated wasn't lost on either of us. I owe her a clean weekend with no obligations. She mentioned wanting to do nothing this Saturday. We will do nothing this Saturday. I am going to make pancakes (her preferred kind, with the lemon zest, not my preferred kind with cornmeal) and we'll watch one of the trashy reality shows she likes that I pretend to dislike but actually enjoy because watching her laugh at the contestants' bad decisions is one of my favorite things in the world. Nube will sleep between us. I will not check Slack. The phone will be face-down. Whole-day commitment.

Work has been weirdly normal. The rust shadow rollout begins Monday. I came back to a clean inbox because Carla and Andrés handled everything during the days I was off. Andrés left a note in my Slack DMs that said 'enjoy the belt, jefe' which is the first time he's called me jefe and I'm pretending I didn't notice while also definitely noticing. Carla just sent the chaos test scenarios for review, no commentary on the belt because Carla is not a commentary-on-personal-events kind of person. Pure Carla. Roberto sent me a one-line congrats — 'felicitaciones por el cinturón Sebastián, listo para el lunes?' — that was so on-brand for him I laughed out loud at my desk and Andrés looked over.

The big thing nobody warned me about: the day after a goal you've worked toward for years, you're a little disoriented in a way that's hard to articulate to people who haven't done it. The fixed point in the future has dissolved. There's no Saturday cruel-classes anymore. There's no testing on the calendar. The thing I was sprinting toward is behind me, not ahead. I have to find the next thing or I'll feel hollow, which is a kind of hollow I haven't felt before because I've always had the next training cycle to point at. Some part of me knows the chess board is the next thing — Hugo's birthday is six weeks out, the inlay phase is up next, Camila's dad cries easy and I want to make him cry on his birthday in the good way. Some part of me knows MIT is the bigger next thing — the application I haven't started writing the personal statement for is sitting in my mind like a heavy object I keep walking past in the apartment, knowing I'll trip over it eventually if I don't move it.

But neither of those is the same shape as the test. The test had a date. The test had a binary outcome — pass or retest. The test was witnessed by people who flew in for it. The chess board is private, an offering between me and Hugo. The MIT app is uncertain — submission is months from result, result is months from any actual change. Different fuel sources entirely. I'm going to have to learn to run on those different fuels, because the test fuel — the calendar event with witnesses — is rare and I can't construct one for every project. Maybe Profe was talking about exactly this in his speech when he mentioned the third-dan and fourth-dan students who keep showing up. The fuel switches at some point from external to internal, and you either learn to run on internal fuel or you stop showing up.

Profe told me on the way out Monday — 'el primer dan no es la cumbre. Es la base.' First dan isn't the peak. It's the base. Five more dans above me potentially. I have years of training ahead. The belt is just a marker that the real work has now started, in the same way that getting hired at your first real job isn't the achievement, it's the entry to the actual work. I think I knew that going in. I think I needed Profe to say it out loud anyway, the way some things only become real when someone you respect names them.

Anyway. Sentimental rambling on a Wednesday morning before standup. Going to bed at a reasonable hour tonight. Insomnia has been better since the test — the body finally relaxed, the cortisol surge that was hitting at 3am has been lighter. I forgot what 7 hours of sleep felt like. Not bad. Going to keep this up, at least until the next thing arrives.

One specific thing I want to write down before it fades: I noticed during Monday's training that I was less anxious about getting hit. Not less attentive — I'm not stupid — but less anxious. Some of the latent test-related tension had bled out of my sparring. I was looser. My defense was more economical. My counter-strikes happened earlier and with less wind-up. Profe noticed and pulled me aside after class to say 'estás más relajado, sigue así.' You're more relaxed, keep it up. The relaxation is the dividend of the test being behind me. The body had been holding tension for months without my noticing the daily cost. The cost was visible in retrospect now that it's gone. I wonder how much better my training will be over the next year now that the body isn't running on test-anticipation for the first time in eighteen months. The next test (segundo dan) is at minimum two years away under Atlas Box rules. So I have a long stretch of training without an external goal pressing on me, and I get to discover what my training looks like in that mode. Profe says it's where the real learning happens — when there's no test in front of you and you're training because the training is the thing. We'll see if I'm built for that mode or if I need a goal in front of me to stay sharp.

Camila ordered a small framed print of one of the photos from the test — the one Pablo took right after Profe handed me the belt, where I'm looking at Profe rather than at the camera and Profe's hand is still on my shoulder. She had it framed at a place near our apartment in Roma Norte, small black wooden frame, simple matting. It sits on the entryway shelf next to the display case for the belt. When you walk into the apartment now, the first thing you see is that shelf. The bombilla cup, the belt, the framed photo of the moment of receiving the belt. Three objects in a row. I keep thinking about how those three objects map onto the three lineages I'm part of — Mama Lupita's family lineage from Guadalajara through to Mexico City, Profe's eight-year coaching arc through Atlas Box, and the moment itself which is the present-tense version of both of those threads converging in a public ceremony. The shelf is a small altar of sorts, though I don't say that out loud because I don't want to dramatize it. Camila understands without me saying it. She sees me touching the shelf as I leave for work and she doesn't comment. The not-commenting is a kind of understanding. Camila looked at the framed photo this morning and said 'qué guapo cuando ganas.' How handsome you look when you win. I told her I look the same when I lose. She said 'no, but I love you for not knowing the difference.' That's going on the t-shirt next to Profe's other lines.

Got back to the chess board today. Hadn't touched it in two weeks because of test prep and family in town. The cedar has settled in the apartment well — no movement that I can see, no visible warping, no joint creep on the squares I'd already cut and laminated. Small mercy. I was half-expecting to come back to a disaster after two weeks of CDMX humidity swings (the building's old radiators do weird things to ambient moisture in the apartment), and instead I came back to wood that had behaved itself. Wood is a more stable material than I'd given it credit for, when you set it up correctly. The hard part is the setup.

Started on the walnut inlay today, which is the part I've been quietly dreading since I started this project because there's no margin for error on the inlay. The walnut is more expensive per square inch than the cedar, and the visual appeal of the whole board depends on the contrast of the dark walnut squares against the warm cedar substrate. One bad cut on a walnut piece and I'm driving back to El Roble in Toluca for more, two hours each way through Friday traffic, and Camila will not be amused at another solo-Sebastián vinyl-listening road trip in La Torta this month, especially not so soon after promising her the no-obligations Saturday.

Spent two hours just measuring and re-measuring before making a single cut, drawing on the walnut with a marking knife along a steel ruler so the lines are sharp and consistent rather than a soft pencil mark that compresses under the chisel. The Veritas chisel I sharpened over the weekend on the diamond stones is taking nice clean shavings, the kind that curl off the edge in continuous ribbons that stay together rather than splintering or breaking. The shop floor at the Roma Norte maker space is now cedar shavings and walnut chips and one stray Nube hair from when she came to visit last week and tried to eat a cedar shaving before I caught her. Hugo's birthday is in 6 weeks. I think I can finish. I have to. The deadline is non-negotiable in a way work deadlines aren't.

The maker space was emptier than usual today. The other regulars were on their own projects in the corner — one guy doing a turned-bowl on the lathe, another doing some carving work with a chisel set I didn't recognize, a couple working together on a small cabinet — and the room had the focused-quiet that good shops have when everyone is in their own task. I'd missed the room for two weeks. The room is part of the work. The other woodworkers don't talk to me about my project but they nod when I come in and nod when I leave, and that's the right level of community for what I want from the room. Not friends. Not strangers. Co-occupants of a shared space who recognize that we're all there for the same reason — to make something that will outlast the day's labor. Hugo's birthday in 6 weeks. I left at 9pm with the first walnut piece roughed out and clamped under cauls to settle overnight. Tomorrow morning I'll cut the second piece. The pace is established.

Tomorrow I'm doing the second walnut piece. Maker space at 7pm after dinner, three hours of cutting and shaping with no music in the headphones because the work needs the sound of the tools — the saw, the chisel, the occasional curse — to focus. Hugo's birthday in 6 weeks. The pacing is fine. The pacing is also non-negotiable. The board has to be done.

Three days in a row of 6+ hours of sleep. Body is finally catching up to the absence of test pressure. The 3am wake hasn't completely stopped — last night I woke at 3:50am and lay there for fifteen minutes — but I rolled over and was back asleep within those fifteen minutes, which has been impossible for months. The wake is shorter and shallower now, more like a brief surfacing than a full wake. Whatever was driving it isn't driving as hard now that the test is behind me.

Back is at maybe 60% — best it's been since month 10 when I tweaked it sparring during the Tijuana-belt-incident week. PT exercises continuing but I've cut down to twice a week from three because Dra. Reyes thinks the active load from kickboxing post-test will be lower for a couple of months and we can use that window to consolidate gains rather than just maintain. She says we can probably wean off PT entirely in another month if I keep up the home routine — single-leg bridges, dead bugs, the resistance band exercise I still hate, the cat-cow stretches. The home routine takes 12 minutes morning and 12 minutes evening and I've gotten consistent enough about it that the body now expects it.

Camila says I look less haunted in the morning. I had not realized I looked haunted in the morning. The body remembers stress even when the mind has moved on, apparently — for weeks the mind was telling me 'we're fine, the test is going well' and meanwhile my face was telling Camila something else entirely at 6am over coffee. She didn't say anything at the time because she didn't want to add to the stress by pointing it out. Now that the stress is receding she can mention it as a past-tense observation. I'll take her observation as data. The body knows. The face knows. The closest observer knows.

What I want to track, methodically: the sleep is improving but the underlying causes I identified at 3am — the chronic-stress framing, the never-bored thing, the optimization of slack out of my mental life — those haven't gone away. The test pressure released and the symptom subsided, which is consistent with the chronic-stress hypothesis but doesn't prove it. The next stress event will be a test of whether I've changed anything structural or just had a temporary reprieve. The MIT essay is still ahead. The chess board deadline is closing in. The rust prod rollout is in a critical week. Camila's deadline at her firm is in two weeks. So I have a stress portfolio of medium-grade items lining up for the next month. If the sleep stays good through that, I'll consider it real recovery. If it goes back to 3am wakes when the next deadline gets close, then the test was just the surface symptom and the underlying pattern is still there. Going to keep the sleep notebook. Will know in three weeks.

The thing I'm watching most carefully is whether I can hold this sleep recovery while the rust prod rollout is in its critical window. The rollout starts Monday. If I can sleep through the rollout's most stressful days, that's actual data about whether my body has reset. If the 3am wake comes back the night before week 2 of the rollout when we're moving from 1% to 50% traffic, then I'll know the test was just the surface and the underlying stress response is unchanged. Empirical experiment running. Will know within three weeks.

Shadow rollout day 1. Watching the dashboard with the kind of attention reserved for childbirth. Carla is on a video call from her desk at home (her partner has the baby this morning), Andrés is in the office bullpen with two monitors set up specifically for the rollout, I'm in the corner office with two screens — one for Grafana with the latency and error rate panels pinned, one for the diff worker output streaming the per-request comparison results in a tail -f window. Roberto is in his office across the hall pretending to do other work and refreshing the rollout page every two minutes. He came over once already at 11am to ask 'is it working?' and I said yes and he said 'good' and walked back. Twice more before lunch. By 2pm he'd settled and stopped coming over. Healthy detachment achieved.

Summary so far, four hours in:

- 100% of prod traffic mirrored successfully via the shadow proxy. No origin server impact (the shadow proxy buffers and fires async with a bounded queue, exactly as designed). Zero customer-visible latency increase. The mirror itself is invisible to clients.
- Rust pod CPU steady at 28% under our normal Tuesday afternoon load. Memory flat over four hours, no growth visible in the heap RSS metric, no growth in the active-tasks gauge from tokio-metrics. The multi-thread runtime is humming without complaint.
- p50 / p95 / p99 on rust path matching staging numbers within 2ms across the board. Beautiful. The metrics chart is so clean it looks fake — flat lines where production usually has texture, because the rust path is consistently faster than any tail load on Python.
- Diff worker is reporting 99.7% match rate on dispatch decisions. The 0.3% delta is exclusively in the multi-stop optimization where Python and Rust break ties differently. Carla's now wondering if our seed-based determinism has a bug. We're investigating in parallel with the rollout, not blocking on it.
- Healthz under shadow load is returning in <1ms consistently, exactly like staging — which is itself a meaningful signal because healthz behavior under real prod load shape was the failure mode we'd discovered a month ago and it has stayed clean throughout four hours of real traffic.

One genuinely interesting catch: at the 90-minute mark we saw a single rust pod's task count climb to 4x baseline for about 8 seconds — went from ~120 active tasks to ~500. No latency impact (stayed within p99 SLO), no errors, no memory spike. Just a visible spike on the tokio-metrics task gauge that I noticed because I happened to be looking at that panel.

Carla dug in immediately, the way only Carla does — pulled the corresponding logs by timestamp, joined them against the request log, identified the customer ID. Turned out to be one of our enterprise customers (a Mexican retail chain whose name I won't say in chat for confidentiality) that fired 2k requests in a single batch at us, which is well within their rate limit but unusual at that concentration. Their batch processor must have flushed all at once, probably a deployment or a scheduled job kicking off. Rust handled it elegantly. Multi-thread runtime ate the burst, scheduled the work across all 4 worker threads, and went back to baseline in under 10 seconds. Healthz never blipped. The same burst on the old current_thread setup would have starved the runtime — exactly the failure mode we hit in load test back in month 14. So we already have a real-world receipt for the runtime fix on day one of shadow, with real customer traffic. Carla and I high-fived over slack with the high-five emoji which is the closest we get to celebration during work hours.

The diff-worker investigation: I'm pretty sure the 0.3% mismatch is real, not a determinism bug in our seed propagation. The Python tie-breaker uses Python's `random.choice` with a seeded `random.Random` instance. The Rust side uses `rand::rngs::SmallRng::seed_from_u64`. Both seeded with the comparison_seed header that the shadow proxy sets. But the underlying PRNGs are different algorithms — Python uses Mersenne Twister (MT19937), SmallRng uses xoshiro128++. Same seed, different output. So even with identical seeds, the random number streams diverge after the first call, and any time the dispatch heuristic hits a tie that needs a coin flip, the two implementations may flip differently.

Embarrassing that we didn't catch this in staging — we caught it on day one of shadow because the diff worker ran against thousands of real requests with realistic tie distributions, instead of the synthetic test cases we'd built for staging which had less ambiguous inputs and rarely triggered tie-breaks.

We can't make these match without either (a) reimplementing Mersenne Twister in rust on the dispatch path, which is a non-trivial cost both in implementation effort and in runtime overhead since MT is slower than xoshiro, or (b) accepting the 0.3% delta as 'expected non-determinism, both decisions equally valid by the dispatch heuristic.' We're going with (b) and documenting it in the rollout runbook. Andrés is going to write the explainer for the broader engineering team so nobody panics when they see the diff number in the daily shadow report and assumes there's a bug.

Carla also flagged something subtle that I would have missed: the diff distribution within the 0.3% mismatch is uniform across customers and request shapes, which is what you'd expect from a tie-break difference — random distribution across customers. If it were a logic bug we'd expect it to cluster around specific request shapes or customer IDs (like 'all customers with >20 stops' or 'all requests with backhaul flag set'). So the uniformity is itself evidence that the 0.3% is genuinely just RNG drift, not a real divergence in dispatch quality. I'm going to add this observation to the runbook explainer because it's the kind of subtle reasoning that other teams can borrow when they do similar comparisons.

Going to keep watching the dashboard for another two hours then break for the 7pm sparring session at Atlas Box. First training session as a black belt. I keep forgetting and then remembering and grinning at the screen like an idiot. Carla noticed the third time and asked what I was grinning at. I told her. She said 'felicidades, ahora vuelve al trabajo.' Congratulations, now back to work. Same thing Profe would have said in the same circumstance. I love working with adults who can compress an emotion and a redirect into one sentence.

End of day stats, for the daily report I have to send Roberto and the engineering org tomorrow morning:

- Total mirrored requests: 8.4M over 9 hours.
- Diff worker comparisons completed: 84,000 (1% sample as planned).
- Match rate on dispatch decisions: 99.7% (consistent with the early-day number).
- p50 / p95 / p99 on rust path: 22ms / 71ms / 142ms.
- p50 / p95 / p99 on python path (live, baseline): 28ms / 119ms / 287ms.
- Error rate on rust path: 0.011% (one bad request from a malformed customer payload that we'll handle in the runbook).
- Error rate on python path (live): 0.034%.
- Memory footprint per rust pod: stable at 380MB across all three pods.
- CPU utilization per rust pod: 24-31% across the day, peaking at 31% during the 2pm secondary dispatch window.

The numbers are better than I expected. Significantly better on the rust path across every percentile. We knew rust would be faster, but the absolute deltas — 6ms on p50, 48ms on p95, 145ms on p99 — are bigger than the staging numbers predicted. Production traffic shape must be more pathological than our staging synthetic load. Possibly the geometry decode CPU work, which was the thing that triggered runtime starvation in the first place, has more variance in real customer data than we were modeling. The rust path is handling that variance much more gracefully than Python ever did. That gracefully is going to translate into lower P99 outliers for customers, which is going to show up in their internal dashboards as 'dispatch is faster' which is the kind of thing that gets noticed even when nobody's looking specifically for it.

Closing the laptop now. Going to Atlas Box. First night training as a black belt. Profe will treat me like a regular Monday because he's already told me he will, and that's right. The belt is on the wall at home in Camila's display case. The work continues. The rollout continues tomorrow. Every metric pointing the right direction. Cautious optimism. The kind of cautious optimism that earned a high-five emoji from Carla, which is the highest possible cautious-optimism celebration in our team. I'll take it.

Carla mentioned in our evening call that she was proud of how the team executed today. Carla doesn't say things like 'proud' easily. The compliment landed. I told her 'we built this together over months and the rollout is the test of whether we built it right' and she said 'yeah, we built it right, and the rollout is showing it.' Andrés saw the slack thread later and added a fire emoji which is his version of clapping. Roberto saw the same thread and replied 'felicitaciones, mañana otro día.' Congrats, tomorrow another day. He's right. The rollout has 27 more days to run. Today was day 1. We can't celebrate yet because the work isn't done. But we can register that day 1 went well and use that as fuel for the days that come. Tomorrow's plan: validate the day-one numbers continue to hold, run the chaos pod-kill at noon, watch healthz behavior under a kill, write the chaos test up for the wiki Carla wants. Going home now. Camila is making pasta. The dog needs a walk. Day one done.
